Set y = 0 and we have a dense orbit. Applying the baker’s map enough times ( x ,   y ) =   0.   q ˜ 1 1   p 1 1   …   q ˜ 4 1   p 4 1   q ˜ 1 2   p 1 2   …   q ˜ 9 2   p 9 2 … B n ( x ,   y ) =   …   q ˜ i j ⋅ p i j …   =   ( 0.   p i j … , 0.   q i j … ) will get to any grid point with some junk on the end of the binary representation. The distance between grid points goes to zero, so this orbit can get arbitrarily close to any point in the unit square within a finite number of steps. The orbit it dense. 12.1.7 First, we need to make a function definition for the map. The square will have corners (±1, ±1) to make scaling and shifting easier. Scale the square by a factor of w in the x direction and h in the y direction. Instead of making the horseshoe shape, we’re going to make two copies. The top and bottom copy correspond to −1 ≤ x < 0 and 0 ≤ x ≤ 1 respectively. We’re also going to flip the top piece in both directions before shifting up. Next, we shift both the pieces to the left. And finally chop off the excess. The series of steps F ( x ,   y )   =   { x ′   =   w x x ″   =   − x ′ x ‴   =   x ″ − s x − 1 ≤ x < 0 y ′   =   h y y ″   =   − y ′ + s y y ‴   =   y ″ x ′   =   w x x ″   =   x ′ x ‴   =   x ″ − s x 0 ≤ x ≤ 1 y ′   =   h y y ″   =   y ′ − s y y ‴   =   y ″ Putting all the steps together F ( x ,   y )   =   { − w x − s x − 1 ≤ x < 0 − h y + s y w x − s x 0 ≤ x ≤ 1 h y − s y But this is actually missing the chopping-off step. We can restrict the mapping to lie inside the square instead and not worry about the original. (This is assuming s y isn’t too big to chop off anything in the vertical direction.) x n   +   1   =   ± w x n − s x             y n   +   1   =   ± h y n   ∓   s y                         − 1 ≤ x n   +   1 =   ± w x n − s x ≤ 1           − 1 ≤ w x n − s x ≤ 1             − 1 ≤ w x n   +   s x ≤ 1 − 1   +   s x w ≤ x n ≤ 1 + s x w             − 1 − s x w ≤ x n ≤ 1 − s x w It looks like the only points that survived from the original square are in two vertical strips. Next we can find the inverse map explicitly. x n   =   ± x n   +   1 + s x w         y n   =   ± y n   +   1 + s y h We picked w = 5 h   =   1 5 s x = 2 s y   =   2 5 so that the map came out symmetrically. a) Applying the map and then the inverse while remembering to chop off the excess gives b) c) Continuing gives Note: F 3 and F 4 are different, but the detail is too small to see. The color was also changed to black to show detail. If we continue, remembering to chop off the excess at every application of F and F −1 to the square, we’ll get the points that never get chopped off. The invariant set of the map is the Cartesian product of the Cantor set with itself, also known as Cantor dust . Intuitively, this occurs because F and F −1 look just like each other but rotated one-quarter turn
